title: Contrast, Concession & Listing Reasons — のに / ても / し

meta.focus: Express 'even though', 'even if', and list multiple reasons with のに, ても, and し.

sections.title: Contrast, Concession & Listing Reasons
sections.summary: Sometimes the world doesn't go as expected — and Japanese has elegant tools for expressing that. In this lesson you'll learn three important patterns: のに for surprising or unfair contrasts, ても for expressing 'even if' conditions, and し for stacking multiple reasons together.
sections.whyItMatters: These three patterns appear constantly in everyday Japanese — in complaints, encouragement, explanations, and persuasion. Mastering them will make your speech sound natural and expressive.
youWillLearn: のに — 'even though / despite' for unexpected or disappointing contrasts
ても — 'even if / even though' for concessive conditions
し — 'and also' for listing multiple reasons or qualities
How のに differs from ので (contrast vs reason)
How ても differs from てもいい (concession vs permission)

sections.explanation: のに follows a plain-form clause to express that what happened (or didn't happen) was surprising, disappointing, or contrary to expectations. It carries a tone of complaint, frustration, or regret — the speaker felt the outcome was unfair or unexpected.
notes: Before のに, use plain form rules: verb (dictionary, past, negative), i-adjective (plain form), な-adjective + な, noun + な.
Noun + なのに: 先生なのに (even though he's a teacher). Na-adjective + なのに: 大切なのに (even though it's important).
のに expresses the speaker's emotional reaction — surprise, disappointment, or frustration. It is less common in neutral factual statements.
Compare with ので (G17): ので explains the reason FOR something; のに shows that the actual result was contrary to what was expected.

examples.en: Even though I studied, I didn't pass the exam.
examples.breakdown: Plain past 勉強した + のに expresses frustration that studying didn't lead to passing.

examples.en: Even though the weather is nice, I can't go outside.
examples.breakdown: Plain i-adjective (いい) + のに — the speaker is frustrated that nice weather is being wasted.

examples.en: Even though he's a teacher, he made a mistake.
examples.breakdown: Noun + なのに — the copula だ becomes な before のに, just as before ので.

sections.explanation: ても (te-form + も) expresses that the outcome holds regardless of the action or state. Unlike のに, ても is neutral — it doesn't carry a tone of complaint. It sets up a condition and states that the result is unaffected by it.
notes: Verb ても: te-form + も. 食べても (even if [I] eat), 行っても (even if [I] go).
I-adjective ても: adverbial form (～く) + ても. 難しくても (even if it's difficult), 高くても (even if it's expensive).
Na-adjective / noun + でも: 雨でも (even if it rains), 先生でも (even if [you're] a teacher).
たとえ～ても reinforces the concession: たとえ難しくても (even if it is difficult).
Be careful: ても alone means 'even if.' てもいい is a different construction meaning 'it's okay to / permission.' ても alone never expresses permission.

parts.gloss: is empty / still hungry (ている state)

examples.en: Even if I eat, I'm still hungry.
examples.breakdown: Te-form (食べて) + も. The outcome (still hungry) is unaffected by the action (eating).

examples.en: Even if it's difficult, I won't give up.
examples.breakdown: I-adjective adverbial form (難しく) + ても. Difficulty doesn't change the resolve.

examples.en: Even if it rains, I'll go.
examples.breakdown: Noun (雨) + でも expresses the same concessive meaning as ても for verbs. Use でも with nouns and na-adjective stems.

sections.meaning: and also ~ / not only X but also Y (listing reasons)
sections.explanation: し attaches to the end of a clause to list multiple reasons or qualities that all contribute to an overall conclusion. Each し-clause adds to the list, and the final clause states the conclusion or result.
notes: し follows plain form in casual speech, but polite form + し is also natural and common.
The qualities listed with し can be positive or negative, and they all support the same conclusion.
A single し is also valid: 安いし、買おう (It's cheap, so let's buy it) — one reason leading to a decision.
Distinguish し from から (single reason, stronger cause-effect) and ので (formal single reason): し implies multiple contributing factors.

examples.en: It's cheap, and it's also delicious, so I like this shop.
examples.breakdown: Two し-clauses list positive qualities; the conclusion 好きです follows naturally.

examples.en: Today I'm tired, and I was busy, so I want to go home early.
examples.breakdown: Two し-clauses list reasons for wanting to leave. し can follow plain past verbs and adjectives.

examples.en: Japanese is difficult, and it takes time — but it's fun!
examples.breakdown: し lists challenges, then でも introduces a contrasting positive conclusion.

sections.title: のに vs ので — Contrast vs Reason

items.label: のに
points: Expresses an unexpected or unfair contrast
Carries emotional nuance: complaint, frustration, or surprise
The result goes against what was expected
Tone: 'Despite X, the unfair/surprising Y happened'

example.en: Even though I studied, I don't understand. (frustrating!)

items.label: ので
points: No emotional nuance — just cause and effect
The result follows naturally from the reason
Tone: 'Because of X, Y naturally follows'

example.en: Because there's an exam, I'll study. (neutral explanation)

sections.tip: Ask yourself: 'Am I expressing a surprise or complaint, or just giving a reason?' → Surprise or complaint = のに. Neutral reason = ので.

sections.title: ても vs てもいい — Concession vs Permission

items.label: ても
points: Expresses that the outcome holds regardless of the condition
Used to show that X doesn't change Y

example.en: Even if it's difficult, I'll continue.

items.label: てもいい
points: Means 'it's okay to / you may / it's fine if'
The いい signals that the action is acceptable or allowed
G23 covers this pattern in detail

parts.gloss: it's okay to / you may sit

sections.tip: ても alone = 'even if.' てもいい = 'it's okay to.' The addition of いい transforms concession into permission.

sections.title: Real Contexts: のに, ても, and し

examples.context: りきぞ is stuck at home on a sunny day (のに)
examples.en: Even though the weather is nice today, I'm stuck at home.
examples.note: のに carries frustration — the speaker expected to go out but can't.

examples.context: けん encourages りきぞ before an exam (ても)
examples.en: Even if it's difficult, don't give up.
examples.note: ても is neutral — no complaint, just stating a condition that doesn't change the advice.

examples.context: りきぞ recommends a restaurant to けん (し)
examples.en: That shop is cheap, and it's delicious, and the service is great too!
examples.note: し stacks multiple positive qualities — each し-clause adds to the recommendation.

sections.title: After the Exam
sections.context: けん and りきぞ are at a restaurant after a tough exam day. They reflect on the day using のに, し, and ても.

lines.jp: 今日は天気がいいのに、試験がありましたね。
lines.en: Even though the weather is nice today, we had an exam.

lines.jp: そうですね。試験は難しかったし、時間もなかったし、つかれました。
lines.en: You're right. The exam was difficult, and there was no time, so I'm tired.

lines.jp: つかれても、勉強はよかったですよ。
lines.en: Even if you're tired, the studying was worth it.

lines.jp: この店は安いし、おいしいし、大好きです。
lines.en: This shop is cheap, it's delicious, so I love it.

sections.title: Choose the Right Connector
sections.instructions: Select のに, ても, し, or ので to complete each sentence correctly.

items.translation: Even though I practice every day, I don't get better.
items.explanation: のに is correct — the speaker practiced (expected result: improvement), but the actual outcome is contrary and frustrating. ので would give a neutral cause-and-effect reason, not express a complaint.

items.translation: Even if it's difficult, I won't give up.
items.explanation: ても after the adverbial form (難しく) of an i-adjective means 'even if it's difficult.' The outcome (not giving up) holds regardless of the difficulty. のに would imply an unexpected or unfair result, which doesn't fit a general resolution.

items.after: 、サービスもいいし、また来たいです。
items.translation: This shop is cheap, the service is also good, and I want to come again.
items.explanation: し lists multiple positive reasons: 安いし (it's cheap) and サービスもいいし (the service is also good) both contribute to wanting to return. ので would give only a single cause; のに would imply a complaint.

items.translation: Because I'm tired, I want to go home early.
items.explanation: ので gives a neutral, logical reason: being tired → wanting to go home is a natural cause-and-effect. のに would imply a surprising or unfair contrast. し would list multiple reasons rather than provide a single natural cause.

sections.title: Practice: のに, ても, and し
sections.instructions: Choose the best answer for each question.

items.q: Which pattern expresses frustration that effort did not lead to the expected result?
items.explanation: のに expresses that the actual result was contrary to expectations — often with frustration or disappointment. 勉強したのに合格しなかった = Even though I studied, I didn't pass.

items.q: Which is the correct way to say 'Even if it rains, I'll go' (雨 = rain, a noun)?
items.explanation: For nouns, the concessive pattern is noun + でも. 雨でも = even if it rains. Verb ても uses te-form + も; nouns and na-adjective stems use でも instead.

items.q: Which sentence correctly uses し to list reasons?
choices: 安いのに、おいしいし、好きです。
安いし、おいしいし、好きです。
安くても、おいしいし、好きです。
安いから、おいしいので、好きです。
items.answer: 安いし、おいしいし、好きです。
items.explanation: し follows each clause to list multiple reasons: 安いし (it's cheap) + おいしいし (it's also delicious) leads to the conclusion 好きです. Both し-clauses contribute additively to the same conclusion.

items.q: What is the key difference between のに and ので?
choices: のに is polite; ので is casual.
のに contrasts expected vs actual outcomes with emotional nuance; ので gives a neutral reason.
のに is for nouns; ので is for verbs.
items.answer: のに contrasts expected vs actual outcomes with emotional nuance; ので gives a neutral reason.
items.explanation: ので (G17) neutrally explains a cause. のに adds emotional weight — the result was unexpected or felt unfair. 疲れたので帰ります (I'm going home because I'm tired) vs 疲れたのに帰れない (Even though I'm tired, I can't go home — frustrating!).

items.q: How is ても formed with a verb?
items.explanation: ても is the te-form followed by the particle も. First conjugate the verb to its て-form, then add も. 食べ + て + も → 食べても (even if [I] eat). This is why it's written as ても — it literally combines て and も.
